Real Analysis

Finite and Infinite Sets

Finite and Infinite Sets

Basic Definitions

We begin by making precise what it means for a set to have a certain number of elements, and then extend this idea to the infinite case.

Empty Set

The empty set, denoted ∅\emptyset∅, is the unique set containing zero elements.

nnn-Element Set (Cardinality nnn)

A set SSS has nnn elements (equivalently, ∣S∣=n|S| = n∣S∣=n) if there exists a bijection f:{1,2,…,n}→S.f:\{1,2,\dots,n\}\to S.f:{1,2,…,n}→S.

The empty set and the notion of an nnn-element set together let us define finiteness in general.

Finite and Infinite Sets
  • A set SSS is finite if it is empty, or if there exists n∈Nn\in\mathbb{N}n∈N and a bijection f:{1,2,…,n}→Sf:\{1,2,\dots,n\}\to Sf:{1,2,…,n}→S.
  • A set is infinite if it is not finite.
Terminology note on “countable”

Be careful not to conflate this with the term countable. In many texts, “countable” means “finite or countably infinite,” and “countably infinite” means “in bijection with N\mathbb{N}N.” By contrast, the definition of an nnn-element set above uses a bijection with the finite set {1,2,…,n}\{1,2,\dots,n\}{1,2,…,n}, not with N\mathbb{N}N itself. Keeping these bijections straight — finite sets biject with initial segments {1,…,n}\{1,\dots,n\}{1,…,n}, countably infinite sets biject with all of N\mathbb{N}N — will matter once we compare sizes of infinite sets.

Subsets of Finite Sets; Supersets of Infinite Sets

With finiteness defined via bijections, a natural question is how it behaves under taking subsets. Intuitively, a piece of a finite collection should still be finite, and anything containing an infinite collection should itself be infinite. The following theorem confirms this intuition and, notably, shows the second bullet follows immediately from the first by contraposition.

Set Sizing

Suppose T⊆ST\subseteq ST⊆S.

  • If SSS is finite, then TTT is finite.
  • If TTT is infinite, then SSS is infinite.
Proof

First bullet, by induction on n=∣S∣n = |S|n=∣S∣.

Base case (n=0n=0n=0): If S=∅S=\emptysetS=∅, then T⊆ST\subseteq ST⊆S forces T=∅T=\emptysetT=∅, which is finite by definition.

Inductive step: Assume the claim holds for every set of size kkk, and let ∣S∣=k+1|S| = k+1∣S∣=k+1. Fix a bijection f:{1,2,…,k+1}→Sf:\{1,2,\dots,k+1\}\to Sf:{1,2,…,k+1}→S, and set s=f(k+1)s = f(k+1)s=f(k+1). Removing this element, let S′=S∖{s}S' = S \setminus \{s\}S′=S∖{s}, so that ∣S′∣=k|S'| = k∣S′∣=k and the inductive hypothesis applies to S′S'S′.

Now take any T⊆ST \subseteq ST⊆S. There are two cases, depending on whether TTT contains the distinguished element sss.

  • Case 1: s∉Ts \notin Ts∈/T. Then T⊆S′T \subseteq S'T⊆S′ directly, so TTT is finite by the inductive hypothesis.
  • Case 2: s∈Ts \in Ts∈T. Then T∖{s}⊆S′T \setminus \{s\} \subseteq S'T∖{s}⊆S′, so by the inductive hypothesis T∖{s}T\setminus\{s\}T∖{s} is finite — say g:{1,2,…,m}→T∖{s}g:\{1,2,\dots,m\}\to T\setminus\{s\}g:{1,2,…,m}→T∖{s} is a bijection. Extend ggg by appending sss at the end: define ga:{1,2,…,m+1}→Tg_a:\{1,2,\dots,m+1\}\to Tga​:{1,2,…,m+1}→T by
ga(i)={g(i),1≤i≤m,s,i=m+1.g_a(i) = \begin{cases} g(i), & 1 \le i \le m, \\ s, & i = m+1. \end{cases}ga​(i)={g(i),s,​1≤i≤m,i=m+1.​

Since ggg is a bijection onto T∖{s}T\setminus\{s\}T∖{s} and s∉T∖{s}s\notin T\setminus\{s\}s∈/T∖{s}, gag_aga​ is a bijection onto TTT, so TTT is finite.

In either case TTT is finite, completing the inductive step and hence the proof of the first bullet.

Second bullet, by contrapositive of the first: if SSS were finite, the first bullet would force every subset T⊆ST \subseteq ST⊆S — in particular our given TTT — to be finite, contradicting the assumption that TTT is infinite. Hence SSS must be infinite. ■\blacksquare■

Why the two bullets are “the same fact”

The second bullet isn’t an independent result — it’s the contrapositive of the first, restated in terms of infinite sets. This is a pattern worth internalizing: statements about infinite sets are frequently proved by assuming finiteness and deriving a contradiction with a finite-set result you already have.
